ground_truth: Before doing anything else: you want a lawyer involved right from the beginning, to make sure that something reasonable happens with the house if one of you dies or leaves. Seriously, you'll both be safer and happier if it's all explicit. How much you should put on the house is not the right question. Houses don't sell instantly, and while you can access some of their stored value by borrowing against them that too can take some time to arrange. You need to have enough operating capital for normal finances, plus an emergency reserve to cover unexpectedly being out of work or sudden medical expenses. There are suggestions for how much that should be in answers to other questions. After that, the question is whether you should really be buying a house at all. It isn't always a better option than renting and (again as discussed in answers to other questions) there are ongoing costs in time and upkeep and taxes and insurance. If you're just thinking about the financials, it may be better to continue to rent and to invest the savings in the market. The time to buy a house is when you have the money and a reliable income, plan not to move for at least five years, really want the advantages of more elbow room and the freedom to alter the place to suit your needs (which will absorb more money)...  As far as how much to put down vs. finance: you really want a down payment of at least 20%. Anything less than that, and the bank will insist you pay for mortgage insurance, which is a significant expense. Whether you want to pay more than that out of your savings depends on how low an interest rate you can get (this is a good time in that regard) versus how much return you are getting on your investments, combined with how long you want the mortgage to run and how large a mortgage payment you're comfortable committing to. If you've got a good investment plan in progress and can get a mortgage which charges a lower interest rate than your investments can reasonably be expected to pay you, putting less down and taking a larger mortgage is one of the safer forms of leveraged investing... IF you're comfortable with that. If the larger mortgage hanging over you is going to make you uncomfortable,  this might not be a good answer for you. It's a judgement call. I waited until i'd been in out of school about 25 years before I was ready to buy a house. Since i'd been careful with my money over that time,  I had enough in investments that I could have bought the house for cash. Or I could have gone the other way and financed 80% of it for maximum leverage. I decided that what I was comfortable with was financing 50%. You'll have to work thru the numbers and decide what you are comfortable with. But I say again, if buying shared property you need a lawyer involved. It may be absolutely the right thing to do ... but you want to make sure everything is fully spelled out... and you'll also want appropriate terms written into your wills. (Being married would carry some automatic assumptions about joint ownership and survivor rights... but even then it's safer to make it all explicit.) Edit: Yes, making a larger down payment may let you negotiate a lower interest rate on the loan. You'll have to find out what each bank is willing to offer you, or work with a mortgage broker who can explore those options for you.

<think>
The user is asking "How much money should I put on a house?" This is a personal finance question about down payments on a home purchase.

Let me think through what a comprehensive answer should cover:

1. First, I should consider the context - the phrase "put on a house" suggests they might be buying with someone else (like a partner), which raises legal considerations about shared property ownership.

2. The core question about down payment amount needs to be addressed, but there are prerequisite considerations:
   - Do they have enough emergency savings first?
   - Is buying a house even the right decision vs. renting?
   - What's their financial situation overall?

3. For the actual down payment question:
   - The 20% threshold is important because below that, you typically need to pay Private Mortgage Insurance (PMI), which adds significant cost
   - Beyond 20%, it becomes a calculation of opportunity cost - mortgage interest rate vs. investment returns
   - Personal comfort with debt levels matters too

4. If buying with a partner (which the question might imply), legal protections are crucial - having a lawyer draft agreements about what happens if someone dies or the relationship ends.

5. Other factors to consider:
   - How long they plan to stay (typically need 5+ years to make buying worthwhile)
   - Ongoing costs of homeownership (maintenance, taxes, insurance)
   - Their income stability
   - Current interest rate environment

6. The answer should acknowledge this is ultimately a personal judgment call based on individual circumstances, risk tolerance, and financial goals.

Let me structure a comprehensive response that addresses all these angles while being practical and helpful.

ground_truth: If psychologically there is no difference to you between cash and debit (you should test this over a couple of months on yourself and spouse to make sure), then I suggest two debit cards (one for you and spouse) on your main or separate checking account. If you use Mint you can set budgets for each category (envelope) and when a purchase is made Mint will automatically categorize that transaction and deduct that amount from the correct budget.  For example: If you have a "Fast Food" budget set at $100 per month and you use the debit at McDonalds, Mint should automatically categorize it as "Fast Food" and deduct the amount from the "Fast Food" budget that you set.  If it can't determine a category or gets it wrong, you can just select the proper category. Mint has an iPhone (also Android and Windows phone) app that I find very easy to use. Many people state that they don't have this psychologically difference between spending cash and debit/credit, but I would say that most actually do, especially with small purchases.  It doesn't have anything to do with intellect or knowing that you are actually spending money.  It has more to do with tangibility, and the physical act of handing over cash.  You may not add that soda and candy bar to your purchase if you have visible cash in your wallet that will disappear more quickly.  I lived in Germany for 2 years before debit cards were around or common.  I'm a sharp guy and even though I knew that I paid $100 for the 152 DM, it still kind of felt like spending Monopoly money, especially considering that in the US we are used to coins normally being 25 cents or less and in Germany coins are up to 10 DM (almost $10) and are used more frequently than paper.

The user is asking about a "digital envelope system" which is a modern take on the traditional envelope budgeting method. Let me think through what this question is really asking and how to provide a helpful response.

The traditional envelope system involves putting cash into physical envelopes labeled for different spending categories (groceries, entertainment, gas, etc.). When an envelope is empty, you stop spending in that category. It's a tangible way to control spending.

A "modern take" would be adapting this system for digital/electronic payments since most people don't use cash as much anymore. The question seems to be asking how to implement envelope budgeting in a digital world.

Key considerations for a digital envelope system:

1. **Tools/Apps**: There are budgeting apps like Mint, YNAB (You Need A Budget), EveryDollar, and others that allow you to create virtual "envelopes" or budget categories. These apps can track spending automatically by connecting to bank accounts and credit cards.

2. **Debit vs Credit vs Cash**: One consideration is whether using debit/credit cards provides the same psychological spending control as cash. Research suggests that people tend to spend more freely with cards than cash because of the tangibility factor - physically handing over money creates more "pain of paying."

3. **Implementation options**:
   - Using budgeting software to track categories
   - Having multiple bank accounts for different purposes
   - Using debit cards linked to specific accounts
   - Setting up alerts when approaching budget limits

4. **The psychological aspect**: This is important - the envelope system works partly because of the physical limitation and visibility of cash. Digital systems need to replicate this somehow through alerts, visual progress bars, etc.

5. **Practical advice**: Testing whether you and your spouse actually behave the same with digital payments as with cash is important before fully committing to a digital system.

The response should address how to practically implement envelope budgeting digitally while acknowledging the psychological differences between cash and card spending.

ground_truth: The Fair Credit Reporting Act specifies in some detail on pages 50-54 (as labeled in the footer, 55-59 as pages in pdf) the process that occurs when a consumer initiates a dispute.  The safe outcome for the reporting agency is to remove the information in dispute  from reports within 30 days if the reporting party does not certify the information is complete and accurate (with other statutory timelines for communication to the customer and the reporter).   If you initiate a dispute, then the agency is following the law by deleting the reported information, outside new input from the furnisher.  If this is unsatisfactory, you have the following statutory right within § 611. Procedure in case of disputed accuracy [15 U.S.C. § 1681i (d) Notification of deletion of disputed information. Following any deletion of   information which is found to be inaccurate or whose accuracy can no longer   be verified or any notation as to disputed information, the consumer   reporting agency shall, at the request of the consumer, furnish notification   that the item has been deleted or the statement, codification or summary   pursuant to subsection (b) or (c) of this section to any person specifically   designated by the consumer who has within two years prior thereto received   a consumer report for employment purposes, or within six months   prior thereto received a consumer report for any other purpose, which   contained the deleted or disputed information.  The section that binds furnishers of information (§ 623. Responsibilities of furnishers of information to consumer reporting agencies [15 U.S.C. § 1681s-2], starting on page 78 in the footer) places on them the following specific duties: (B) Reporting information after notice and confirmation of errors.   A person shall not furnish information relating to a consumer to   any consumer reporting agency if   (i) the person has been notified by the consumer, at the address   specified by the person for such notices, that specific   information is inaccurate; and   (ii) the information is, in fact, inaccurate. ... (2) Duty to correct and update information. A person who   (A) regularly and in the ordinary course of business furnishes information   to one or more consumer reporting agencies about the   person’s transactions or experiences with any consumer; and   (B) has furnished to a consumer reporting agency information   that the person determines is not complete or accurate, shall   promptly notify the consumer reporting agency of that determination   and provide to the agency any corrections to that information,   or any additional information, that is necessary to make   the information provided by the person to the agency complete   and accurate, and shall not thereafter furnish to the agency any   of the information that remains not complete or accurate. So there you have it: they have to stop reporting inaccurate information, and "promptly" notify the credit agency once they've determined what is incomplete or inaccurate. I note no specific statutory timeline for this investigation.

<think>
The user is asking whether there are rules against penalizing consumers for requesting accurate credit reporting. This is a question about consumer protection laws related to credit reporting.

Let me think through this systematically:

1. The main federal law governing credit reporting in the United States is the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA), codified at 15 U.S.C. § 1681 et seq.

2. The question is specifically about whether consumers can be penalized for disputing inaccurate information or requesting corrections to their credit reports.

3. Under the FCRA, consumers have the right to dispute inaccurate information on their credit reports. The key provisions I should consider are:

- Section 611 (15 U.S.C. § 1681i) - This covers the procedure for disputed accuracy. When a consumer disputes information, the credit reporting agency must investigate within 30 days (or 45 days in certain circumstances). If the information cannot be verified, it must be deleted.

- Section 623 (15 U.S.C. § 1681s-2) - This covers the responsibilities of furnishers of information. Furnishers have duties to report accurate information and to investigate disputes.

4. The FCRA establishes a framework where:
- Consumers can dispute inaccurate information
- Credit reporting agencies must investigate disputes
- If information cannot be verified, it must be removed
- Furnishers must stop reporting information they know to be inaccurate

5. The law essentially protects consumers' rights to dispute information without penalty. The credit reporting agencies are required to follow specific procedures when disputes are filed, and the "safe" outcome for them is to remove disputed information if it cannot be verified within the statutory timeframe.

6. Section 611(d) also gives consumers the right to have notification of deletions sent to recent recipients of their credit reports.

7. Furnishers are prohibited from continuing to report information that they know is inaccurate after being notified by the consumer.

So to answer the question: Yes, the FCRA provides protections for consumers who dispute information on their credit reports. The law establishes procedures that credit reporting agencies and furnishers must follow, and these procedures are designed to protect consumers' rights to accurate credit reporting without penalizing them for exercising those rights.

ground_truth: 1. Interest rates What you should know is that the longer the "term" of a bond fund, the more it will be affected by interest rates. So a short-term bond fund will not be subject to large gains or losses due to rate changes, an intermediate-term bond fund will be subject to moderate gains or losses, and a long-term bond fund will be subject to the largest gains or losses. When a book or financial planner says to buy "bonds" with no other qualification, they almost always mean investment-grade intermediate-term bond funds (or for individual bonds, the equivalent would be a bond ladder averaging an intermediate term). If you want technical details, look at the "average duration" or "average maturity" of the bond fund; as a rough guide, if the duration is 10, then a 1% change in interest rates would be a 10% gain or loss on the fund. Another thing you can do is look at long-term (10 years or ideally longer) performance history on some short, intermediate, and long term bond index funds, and you can see how the long term funds bounced around more. Non-investment-grade bonds (aka junk bonds or high yield bonds) are more affected by factors other than interest rates, including some of the same factors (economic booms or recessions) that affect stocks. As a result, they aren't as good for diversifying a portfolio that otherwise consists of stocks. (Having stocks, investment grade bonds, and also a little bit in high-yield bonds can add diversification, though. Just don't replace your bond allocation with high-yield bonds.) A variety of "complicated" bonds exist (convertible bonds are an example) and these are tough to analyze. There are also "floating rate" bonds (bank loan funds), these have minimal interest rate sensitivity because the rate goes up to offset rate rises. These funds still have credit risks, in the credit crisis some of them lost a lot of money. 2. Diversification The purpose of diversification is risk control. Your non-bond funds will outperform in many years, but in other years (say the -37% S&P 500 drop in 2008) they may not. You will not know in advance which year you'll get. You get risk control in at least a few ways. There's also an academic Modern Portfolio Theory explanation for why you should diversify among risky assets (aka stocks), something like: for a given desired risk/return ratio, it's better to leverage up a diverse portfolio than to use a non-diverse portfolio, because risk that can be eliminated through diversification is not compensated by increased returns. The theory also goes that you should choose your diversification between risk assets and the risk-free asset according to your risk tolerance (i.e. select the highest return with tolerable risk). See http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modern_portfolio_theory for excruciating detail. The translation of the MPT stuff to practical steps is typically, put as much in stock index funds as you can tolerate over your time horizon, and put the rest in (intermediate-term investment-grade) bond index funds. That's probably what your planner is asking you to do. My personal view, which is not the standard view, is that you should take as much risk as you need to take, not as much as you think you can tolerate: http://blog.ometer.com/2010/11/10/take-risks-in-life-for-savings-choose-a-balanced-fund/ But almost everyone else will say to do the 80/20 if you have decades to retirement and feel you can tolerate the risk, so my view that 60/40 is the max desirable allocation to stocks is not mainstream. Your planner's 80/20 advice is the standard advice. Before doing 100% stocks I'd give you at least a couple cautions: See also:

When a book or financial planner says to buy "bonds" with no other qualification, they almost always mean investment-grade intermediate-term bond funds (or for individual bonds, the equivalent would be a bond ladder averaging an intermediate term). If you want technical details, look at the "average duration" or "average maturity" of the bond fund; as a rough guide, if the duration is 10, then a 1% change in interest rates would be a 10% gain or loss on the fund. Another thing you can do is look at long-term (10 years or ideally longer) performance history on some short, intermediate, and long term bond index funds, and you can see how the long term funds bounced around more. Non-investment-grade bonds (aka junk bonds or high yield bonds) are more affected by factors other than interest rates, including some of the same factors (economic booms or recessions) that affect stocks. As a result, they aren't as good for diversifying a portfolio that otherwise consists of stocks. (Having stocks, investment grade bonds, and also a little bit in high-yield bonds can add diversification, though. Just don't replace your bond allocation with high-yield bonds.) A variety of "complicated" bonds exist (convertible bonds are an example) and these are tough to analyze. There are also "floating rate" bonds (bank loan funds), these have minimal interest rate sensitivity because the rate goes up to offset rate rises. These funds still have credit risks, in the credit crisis some of them lost a lot of money. 2. Diversification The purpose of diversification is risk control. Your non-bond funds will outperform in many years, but in other years (say the -37% S&P 500 drop in 2008) they may not. You will not know in advance which year you'll get. You get risk control in at least a few ways. There's also an academic Modern Portfolio Theory explanation for why you should diversify among risky assets (aka stocks), something like: for a given desired risk/return ratio, it's better to leverage up a diverse portfolio than to use a non-diverse portfolio, because risk that can be eliminated through diversification is not compensated by increased returns. The theory also goes that you should choose your diversification between risk assets and the risk-free asset according to your risk tolerance (i.e. select the highest return with tolerable risk). See http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modern_portfolio_theory for excruciating detail. The translation of the MPT stuff to practical steps is typically, put as much in stock index funds as you can tolerate over your time horizon, and put the rest in (intermediate-term investment-grade) bond index funds. That's probably what your planner is asking you to do. My personal view, which is not the standard view, is that you should take as much risk as you need to take, not as much as you think you can tolerate: http://blog.ometer.com/2010/11/10/take-risks-in-life-for-savings-choose-a-balanced-fund/ But almost everyone else will say to do the 80/20 if you have decades to retirement and feel you can tolerate the risk, so my view that 60/40 is the max desirable allocation to stocks is not mainstream. Your planner's 80/20 advice is the standard advice. Before doing 100% stocks I'd give you at least a couple cautions: See also:

<think>
The user is asking about how bonds would fare if interest rates rose. This is a fundamental question about bond investing and the inverse relationship between interest rates and bond prices.

Let me think through this:

1. **The basic relationship**: When interest rates rise, existing bond prices fall. This is because new bonds will be issued at higher interest rates, making existing bonds with lower rates less attractive. Investors will only buy existing bonds at a discount.

2. **Duration matters**: The impact of interest rate changes depends heavily on the bond's duration or maturity:
   - Short-term bonds are less affected by rate changes
   - Intermediate-term bonds are moderately affected
   - Long-term bonds are most affected
   
   This is because with longer-term bonds, you're locked into the lower rate for a longer period, so the price adjustment needs to be larger.

3. **Duration as a measure**: Duration is a useful metric - roughly speaking, if a bond fund has a duration of 10 years, a 1% rise in interest rates would cause approximately a 10% decline in the fund's value.

4. **Types of bonds**:
   - Investment-grade bonds are primarily affected by interest rate changes
   - High-yield/junk bonds are more affected by credit risk and economic conditions
   - Floating rate bonds have minimal interest rate sensitivity since their rates adjust
   - Convertible and other complex bonds are harder to analyze

5. **Diversification context**: The question might be part of a broader discussion about portfolio allocation. Bonds serve as diversification against stocks, but their behavior during rate changes is important to understand.

So to answer the question directly: If interest rates rose, bond prices would fall. The magnitude of the decline depends on the duration/maturity of the bonds - longer-term bonds would suffer larger losses than shorter-term bonds.
</think>

When interest rates rise, bond prices fall - this is the fundamental inverse relationship in bond investing. Let me work through the key factors:

**Duration/Maturity is Critical**
The longer the term of a bond or bond fund, the more sensitive it is to interest rate changes:
- Short-term bond funds: minimal impact from rate changes
- Intermediate-term bond funds: moderate impact
- Long-term bond funds: largest impact

A useful rule of thumb: if a bond fund has a duration of 10, then a 1% increase in interest rates would result in approximately a 10% loss in the fund's value.

**Why This Happens**
When rates rise, newly issued bonds offer higher yields, making existing bonds with lower rates less attractive. The market price of existing bonds must drop to compensate.

**Different Bond Types React Differently**
- Investment-grade bonds are primarily driven by interest rate movements
- High-yield/junk bonds are more influenced by economic conditions and credit risk (similar factors that affect stocks)
- Floating-rate bonds have minimal interest rate sensitivity since their rates adjust upward with market rates
- Complex bonds (like convertibles) are harder to predict

**Practical Implications**
When financial advisors recommend "bonds" without qualification, they typically mean intermediate-term investment-grade bonds, which offer a balance between yield and interest rate risk. Looking at historical performance of short, intermediate, and long-term bond index funds over 10+ years reveals how long-term funds experience more volatility.

This interest rate sensitivity is why bonds serve as diversification in a portfolio - they often behave differently than stocks during various market conditions.

ground_truth: You are not limited in these 3 choices. You can also invest in ETFs, which are similar to mutual funds, but traded like stocks. Usually (at least in Canada), MERs for ETFs are smaller than for mutual funds.

generation.question: Long term investing alternative to mutual funds
generation.dataset_input: Long term investing alternative to mutual funds
generation.dataset_output: You are not limited in these 3 choices. You can also invest in ETFs, which are similar to mutual funds, but traded like stocks. Usually (at least in Canada), MERs for ETFs are smaller than for mutual funds.
generation.locked_final_answer: You are not limited in these 3 choices. You can also invest in ETFs, which are similar to mutual funds, but traded like stocks. Usually (at least in Canada), MERs for ETFs are smaller than for mutual funds.
generation.raw_text:
<think>
The user is asking about long-term investing alternatives to mutual funds. Let me think about what options exist for someone who wants to invest for the long term but doesn't want to use mutual funds.

First, let me consider why someone might want alternatives to mutual funds:
- Mutual funds often have higher management expense ratios (MERs)
- They may have minimum investment requirements
- They're priced only at end of day
- Some people want more control or flexibility

Common alternatives for long-term investing include:

1. ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds) - These are very similar to mutual funds in that they hold a basket of securities, but they trade on exchanges like stocks. Key advantages:
   - Generally lower MERs/expense ratios compared to mutual funds
   - Can be bought and sold throughout the trading day
   - Often more tax-efficient
   - No minimum investment beyond the price of one share

2. Individual stocks - Direct ownership of company shares

3. Bonds - Fixed income securities

4. Real estate or REITs

5. Index funds (which can be either mutual funds or ETFs)

The most direct and popular alternative that serves a similar purpose to mutual funds would be ETFs. They provide diversification like mutual funds but typically with lower costs. This is particularly relevant in markets like Canada where the difference in MERs between mutual funds and ETFs can be quite significant - Canadian mutual funds are known for having some of the highest fees in the world.

So the key point to make is that ETFs are a strong alternative - they offer similar diversification benefits but with lower fees and more trading flexibility.
</think>

The user is asking about alternatives to mutual funds for long-term investing. Let me think through what options exist that serve a similar purpose.

Mutual funds pool money from investors to buy a diversified portfolio of securities, which is great for long-term investing. The main alternatives that provide similar diversification benefits would include:

1. ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds) - These are essentially similar to mutual funds in structure, holding baskets of securities, but they trade on stock exchanges like individual stocks. This is probably the most direct alternative.

2. Individual stocks - Though this requires more research and doesn't provide automatic diversification.

3. Bonds or bond funds

4. Real estate investments

The most comparable alternative would be ETFs. They offer key advantages over traditional mutual funds:
- They typically have lower management expense ratios (MERs), which is especially notable in markets like Canada where mutual fund fees tend to be quite high
- They can be traded throughout the day like stocks, offering more flexibility
- They often have better tax efficiency

So for someone looking for a long-term investing vehicle similar to mutual funds but potentially with lower costs, ETFs would be the primary recommendation. The fee difference can be significant over time due to compounding, making ETFs an attractive option for cost-conscious long-term investors.

ground_truth: An alternative to paying thousands of dollars for historical prices by the minute:  Subscribe to real time data for as low as USD$1.5/month from your broker, then browse the chart.

generation.question: Historical share price at exact day and time
generation.dataset_input: Historical share price at exact day and time
generation.dataset_output: An alternative to paying thousands of dollars for historical prices by the minute:  Subscribe to real time data for as low as USD$1.5/month from your broker, then browse the chart.
generation.locked_final_answer: An alternative to paying thousands of dollars for historical prices by the minute:  Subscribe to real time data for as low as USD$1.5/month from your broker, then browse the chart.
generation.raw_text:
<think>
The user is asking about how to get historical share price data at an exact day and time. This is a common need for traders, investors, and researchers who want to analyze past market movements at specific moments.

Let me think about what options exist for this:

1. Professional data services - Companies like Bloomberg, Reuters, and various financial data providers offer historical tick-by-tick or minute-by-minute data, but these services are typically very expensive, often costing thousands of dollars per month or requiring expensive terminal subscriptions.

2. Free sources - Yahoo Finance, Google Finance, and similar free services typically only provide daily OHLC (Open, High, Low, Close) data, not intraday historical data at specific times.

3. Broker platforms - Many brokers provide real-time data feeds and charting tools as part of their service. Some offer this for free with an account, while others charge a small monthly fee for real-time data subscriptions.

The practical solution for someone who doesn't want to pay thousands for professional historical data services would be to:
- Subscribe to real-time data through a broker (which is relatively cheap, often just a few dollars per month)
- Use the broker's charting platform to look back at historical intraday data
- Most broker platforms store historical chart data that you can scroll back through

This is a cost-effective workaround because:
- Real-time data subscriptions from brokers are typically very affordable (often $1-5/month for basic market data)
- The charting tools allow you to view historical price movements at various time intervals
- You can manually look up specific dates and times on the chart

This approach makes sense as a practical, budget-friendly alternative to expensive professional data services for someone who just needs to look up occasional historical prices at specific times.
</think>

The user is asking about obtaining historical share price data at a specific day and time, which is a common need for traders and researchers analyzing past market movements.

Let me consider the options available:

1. **Professional data services**: Companies like Bloomberg, Reuters, and specialized financial data providers offer granular historical tick or minute-level data. However, these services are notoriously expensive, often costing thousands of dollars monthly.

2. **Free public sources**: Services like Yahoo Finance or Google Finance typically only provide daily OHLC data, not precise intraday timestamps.

3. **Broker platforms**: Most brokers offer real-time market data subscriptions at very low costs (often just a few dollars per month). Their charting platforms typically store historical intraday data that users can scroll back through.

The practical, cost-effective solution here is to leverage a broker's charting tools. By subscribing to real-time data for a minimal fee (around $1.50/month from some brokers), users gain access to charting functionality that allows them to browse historical price movements at specific times. This is a workaround that avoids the expensive professional data services while still providing access to the granular historical information needed.

This approach works because broker platforms retain historical chart data that can be navigated to find prices at exact moments in time, making it a budget-friendly alternative for occasional lookups.

generation.raw_text:
<think>
The user is asking about whether their stock is gone forever due to a reverse split or company acquisition. This is a general question about what happens to shares in these corporate actions.

Let me think through this:

1. Reverse stock splits: In a reverse split, shareholders receive fewer shares but each share is worth proportionally more. For example, in a 1-for-300 reverse split, 300 shares become 1 share. The total value should theoretically remain the same immediately after the split.

2. However, there's an important consideration - fractional shares. If someone owns fewer shares than the reverse split ratio (e.g., fewer than 300 shares in a 1-for-300 split), they would end up with a fractional share. Companies typically handle this by cashing out shareholders who would receive less than one full share.

3. When a company is bought by another company, shareholders typically receive either:
   - Cash for their shares
   - Shares in the acquiring company
   - A combination of both

The user seems concerned about losing their investment. Based on the question, I should explain what typically happens in these situations.

Looking at this scenario, it appears to involve GT Biopharma Inc. (formerly Oxis International) which did a 1-for-300 reverse stock split as part of a merger. In this case:
- If someone owned 300+ shares, they would receive new shares at the 1:300 ratio
- If someone owned fewer than 300 shares, they would be cashed out - meaning they received money for their shares instead of new shares

So the stock isn't necessarily "gone forever" - it either converted to fewer shares of the new/merged company, or the shareholder was paid cash for their position if they held fewer than the minimum threshold.

The key information to convey is that in a reverse split with a merger, small shareholders often get cashed out rather than receiving fractional shares.
</think>

The user is asking about what happens to their stock after a reverse split or company acquisition. Let me reason through this:

When a company undergoes a reverse stock split, shareholders receive fewer shares but each share is proportionally worth more. For example, in a 1-for-300 reverse split, every 300 shares you owned would become 1 share.

The critical issue here is what happens to shareholders who own fewer shares than the split ratio. Companies typically don't issue fractional shares, so they have to handle these small positions somehow.

In the case of GT Biopharma Inc. (formerly Oxis International, Inc.), they announced a 1-for-300 reverse stock split as part of their merger with Georgetown. This means:

1. Shareholders who owned 300 or more shares would receive 1 new share for every 300 old shares
2. Shareholders who owned fewer than 300 shares would be "cashed out" - meaning they received a cash payment for the value of their shares instead of receiving new shares

So the stock isn't necessarily "gone forever" - it depends on how many shares the person owned. If they had a small position (under 300 shares), the company would have paid them cash for those shares rather than issuing a fractional share. This is a common practice in reverse splits, especially those with large ratios like 1-for-300.

ground_truth: For many people, giving to charity will have minimal effect on their taxes. Non-profits love to attract donations by saying the money is tax deductible, but for most people, it doesn't work out that way. You will only itemize deductions if they exceed your standard deduction. The IRS allows you to either "itemize" your deductions (where you list each deduction you can take) or take the "standard deduction". Consider a married couple filing jointly in 2011. Their standard deduction is $11,400. They are in the 28% tax bracket. They donate $100 of old clothes to the Goodwill, and are looking forward to deducting that on your taxes, and getting $28 of that back. If that's their only deduction, though, they'd have to give up the standard deduction to take the itemized deduction. Not worth it. Suppose instead they have $11,500 of deductions in 2011. Now we're talking, right? No. The tax impact of itemizing is only $28, since they only exceeded the standard deduction by $100. The cost of having a tax accountant fill out the itemization form probably offsets that small gain. There's also all the time that went in to tracking those deductions over the year. Not worth it.  Tax deductions only become worthwhile when they significantly exceed the standard deduction. You need some big ticket items to get past the itemized deduction threshold. For most people, this only happens when they have a mortgage, as the interest on a residence is deductible.  Folks love to suggest that having a mortgage is a good deal, because the interest is deductible. However, since you have to exceed the standard deduction before it makes sense to itemize, it's not likely to be a big win. For most people: TL;DR: Give to charity because you want that charity to have your money. Tax implications are minimal; let your accountant sort it out. Disclaimer: I am not an accountant.

3. How the tax deduction actually works in practice

I should focus on the tax aspect since that's often misunderstood. Many people think that donating to charity means they automatically get money back on their taxes, but that's not quite how it works.

Key points to explain:
- Charitable donations are tax deductible, but only if you itemize deductions
- The IRS gives you a choice: take the standard deduction OR itemize your deductions
- You only benefit from itemizing if your total itemized deductions exceed the standard deduction
- For many people, their charitable donations alone won't push them over the standard deduction threshold

- But if that's their only deduction, they'd be giving up $11,400 standard deduction to claim $100 - that makes no sense
- Even if they have $11,500 in deductions, they only benefit from the $100 that exceeds the standard deduction

The main way people get over the standard deduction threshold is usually through mortgage interest, which can be substantial.

The practical takeaway: People should give to charity because they want to support that cause, not primarily for tax benefits. For most people, the tax implications are minimal unless they have significant itemized deductions already (like mortgage interest).

ground_truth: It is basically the same situation what US was when the crash happened. People took on debt without the means to pay, even with awful credit records. But the problem isn't the debt people take on themselves, but with the limited disposable income they have how efficiently can their debts be serviced. And how do banks who lend out money can recover their money. When banks lend money to all and sundry, they have to take care of defaults and that is when financial wizardry comes into play. In US people have the option to default on their debt and refinance it, so banks assumed default and tried to hedge their risks. If this is an option in Australia, be ready for a crash else not to worry about much. If banks continue lending expect higher inflation rates, higher interest rates and maybe a downgrade of bonds issued by the Australian government. Higher import costs and a boom in exports because of devalued Australian dollar.

generation.raw_text:
<think>
The question asks about the implications of Australia having the highest household debt to disposable income ratio. Let me think through this systematically.

First, I need to understand what a high household debt to disposable income ratio means. This ratio measures how much debt households carry relative to their income after taxes. A high ratio indicates that households are heavily leveraged - they owe a lot compared to what they actually have available to spend or save.

Now, what are the implications of this situation?

1. **Vulnerability to economic shocks**: When households have high debt relative to their disposable income, they have less buffer against economic downturns. If interest rates rise, unemployment increases, or income drops, these households may struggle to service their debts.

2. **Comparison to pre-2008 US situation**: This reminds me of the conditions in the United States before the 2008 financial crisis. In that case, many people took on mortgages and other debts that they couldn't realistically afford, often with poor credit histories. The key issue wasn't just the debt itself, but whether people had sufficient income to service that debt.

3. **Debt servicing capacity**: The critical question is how efficiently can households service their debts with limited disposable income. If a large portion of income goes to debt payments, there's less for consumption, savings, and handling unexpected expenses.

4. **Banking sector risks**: Banks that have lent extensively face risks if borrowers start defaulting. They need to manage these default risks, which can lead to complex financial instruments (like what happened with mortgage-backed securities in the US).

5. **Default options and their impact**: In the US, bankruptcy laws allow people to default and refinance, which changes how banks price risk. If Australia has similar provisions, this could create similar dynamics to what led to the US crash. If not, the situation may be different.

6. **Monetary policy implications**: If banks continue lending despite high debt levels, this could lead to:
   - Higher inflation as more money circulates
   - Higher interest rates as the central bank tries to control inflation
   - Potential downgrades of government bonds if the situation is seen as risky
   - Currency devaluation, which would increase import costs but potentially boost exports

7. **Housing market concerns**: Much of household debt in Australia is tied to mortgages, and high property prices have driven this debt accumulation. A correction in housing prices could leave many households underwater on their mortgages.

The overall implication is that Australia faces significant economic vulnerability due to this high debt ratio, with potential consequences ranging from individual household financial stress to broader macroeconomic instability.
</think>

ground_truth: In day trading, you're trying to predict the immediate fluctuations of an essentially random system.  In long-term investing, you're trying to assess the strength of a company over a period of time. You also have frequent opportunities to assess your position and either add to it or get out.

generation.question: Why is day trading considered riskier than long-term trading?
generation.dataset_input: Why is day trading considered riskier than long-term trading?
generation.dataset_output: In day trading, you're trying to predict the immediate fluctuations of an essentially random system.  In long-term investing, you're trying to assess the strength of a company over a period of time. You also have frequent opportunities to assess your position and either add to it or get out.
generation.locked_final_answer: In day trading, you're trying to predict the immediate fluctuations of an essentially random system.  In long-term investing, you're trying to assess the strength of a company over a period of time. You also have frequent opportunities to assess your position and either add to it or get out.
generation.raw_text:
<think>
The user is asking why day trading is considered riskier than long-term trading. Let me think through this systematically.

Day trading involves buying and selling financial instruments within the same trading day, trying to profit from short-term price movements. Long-term trading (or investing) involves holding positions for extended periods - months or years.

Why would day trading be riskier?

1. Short-term price movements are essentially random - In the short term, stock prices are influenced by countless unpredictable factors: news, sentiment, algorithmic trading, random fluctuations. This makes predicting minute-to-minute or hour-to-hour movements extremely difficult. It's almost like trying to predict noise in a system.

2. Long-term investing relies on fundamentals - Over longer periods, stock prices tend to reflect the underlying value and performance of companies. You can analyze a company's financials, competitive position, management, industry trends, etc. These are more analyzable and predictable than short-term fluctuations.

3. Time to react and adjust - With long-term investing, you have time to assess how your investment is performing. If a company starts showing warning signs, you have opportunities to evaluate and decide whether to add to your position or exit. Day traders must make split-second decisions.

4. Transaction costs - Day traders make many more trades, accumulating fees and commissions that eat into profits.

5. Emotional pressure - The fast pace of day trading can lead to emotional decision-making.

6. Leverage - Day traders often use margin/leverage, amplifying both gains and losses.

7. Market efficiency - In the short term, markets are highly efficient, making it hard to consistently find profitable opportunities.

The core distinction is that day trading tries to predict essentially random short-term fluctuations, while long-term investing allows you to assess company fundamentals over time and gives you multiple opportunities to evaluate and adjust your position.
</think>

Day trading involves attempting to profit from very short-term price movements that occur within a single trading day. The fundamental problem is that these short-term fluctuations are largely driven by random noise, market sentiment, algorithmic trading, and countless unpredictable micro-events. Essentially, you're trying to find patterns in what behaves like a random system over such brief timeframes.

Long-term investing, by contrast, allows you to base decisions on more analyzable factors - a company's financial health, competitive advantages, management quality, industry trends, and overall economic conditions. Over extended periods, stock prices tend to reflect these underlying fundamentals rather than random noise.

Additionally, long-term investors have the advantage of time. They can continuously monitor their investments, observe how companies perform through various market conditions, and make informed decisions about whether to increase their position or exit. This provides multiple checkpoints to reassess and adjust strategy based on new information.

Day traders must make rapid decisions with limited information, often under significant emotional pressure, while long-term investors can take a more measured, analytical approach to building and managing their portfolios.

ground_truth: With number of Banks increasing every country at some point in time adopted an Identification code. In US these are called ABA number because they are allocated by American Bankers Association, in UK Sort Codes ... like wise for other countries. See list here http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bank_code In some countries the numbers are given by Central Bank.   To enable internationl payments, the SWIFT body apart from message formats, allocated a  SWIFT BIC [Bank identification Code] so that Banks can be globally identified. Currently  IBAN being adopted in Europe & Australia to identify an Account [at a Bank] Uniquely across globe. In essence these number help uniquely identify a Location/Bank/Branch. The clearing house route the payments or collection instruments to the correct Bank on the basis of this number.

generation.raw_text:
<think>
The user is asking why routing numbers are called ABA/ABN numbers. Let me think through this.

A routing number is used in banking to identify financial institutions for the purpose of processing transactions like checks, wire transfers, and direct deposits. The question specifically asks about the terminology "ABA/ABN."

ABA stands for American Bankers Association. In the United States, routing numbers are often called ABA numbers because they were developed and are assigned by the American Bankers Association. This system was established in 1910 to help identify the specific financial institution responsible for the payment of a negotiable instrument.

The ABA routing number is a 9-digit code that identifies the bank or credit union. It's used in the US banking system for various transactions.

Now, regarding "ABN" - this might be a slight confusion or typo in the question. ABN could refer to different things in different contexts, but in banking, the more common term is ABA number in the US context.

Different countries have different systems for bank identification:
- In the US: ABA routing numbers (assigned by American Bankers Association)
- In the UK: Sort codes
- Internationally: SWIFT/BIC codes (Bank Identifier Codes)
- In Europe and increasingly globally: IBAN (International Bank Account Number)

The purpose of these numbers is to uniquely identify banks and their branches so that clearing houses and payment systems can route transactions to the correct financial institution. This became necessary as the number of banks grew and there needed to be a standardized way to identify them for processing payments and other financial instruments.

So the answer to why it's called an ABA number is simply because the American Bankers Association created and administers this numbering system in the United States.
</think>

The routing number is called an ABA number because it is allocated and administered by the American Bankers Association (ABA) in the United States. As the number of banks increased over time, countries needed standardized identification systems for financial institutions.

Different countries developed their own bank identification systems:
- In the US: ABA numbers (from American Bankers Association)
- In the UK: Sort Codes
- Other countries have their own systems, often administered by central banks

These identification numbers serve a crucial purpose - they help uniquely identify a specific bank location or branch. When payments or collection instruments need to be processed, clearing houses use these numbers to route transactions to the correct financial institution.

For international transactions, additional systems were developed:
- SWIFT BIC (Bank Identifier Code) - allows banks to be identified globally for international payments
- IBAN (International Bank Account Number) - being adopted in Europe and Australia to uniquely identify accounts across the globe

In essence, these routing/identification numbers exist because as banking systems grew more complex with more institutions, there needed to be a standardized way to ensure payments and financial instruments reach the correct destination.

These identification numbers serve a crucial purpose - they help uniquely identify a specific bank location or branch. When payments or collection instruments need to be processed, clearing houses use these numbers to route transactions to the correct financial institution.

For international transactions, additional systems were developed:
- SWIFT BIC (Bank Identifier Code) - allows banks to be identified globally for international payments
- IBAN (International Bank Account Number) - being adopted in Europe and Australia to uniquely identify accounts across the globe

In essence, these routing/identification numbers exist because as banking systems grew more complex with more institutions, there needed to be a standardized way to ensure payments and financial instruments reach the correct destination.

ground_truth: You need to pay off the entire balance of 7450 as soon as possible. This should be your primary financial goal at this point above anything else. A basic structure that you can follow is this: Is the £1500 balance with the 39.9% interest rate the obvious starting point here? Yes, that is fine. But all the cards and overdraft debts need to be treated with the same urgency! What are the prospects for improving my credit score in say the next 6-12 months enough to get a 0% balance transfer or loan for consolidation? This should not be a primary concern of yours if you want to move on with your financial life. Debt consolidation will not help you achieve the goals you have described (home ownership, financial stability). If you follow the advice here, by the time you get to the point of being eligible, you may not see enough savings in interest to make it worth the hassle. Focus on the hard stuff and pay off the balances. Is that realistic, or am I looking at a longer term struggle? You are looking at a significant struggle. If it was easy you would not be asking this question! The length of time will be determined by your choices: how aggressively you will cut your lifestyle, take on extra jobs, and place additional payments on your debt.  By being that extreme, you will actually start to see progress, which will be encouraging. If you go in half-committed, your progress will show as much and it will be demotivating. Much of your success will hinge on your mental and emotional toughness to push through the hard work of delaying pleasure and paying off these balances. That is just my personal experience, so you can take it or leave it. :) The credit score will take care of itself if you follow this method, so don't worry about it. Good Luck!

<think>
The user is asking about how to tackle debt when they have high credit utilization and some high interest rates, but their credit score isn't terrible. Let me think through this situation.

First, I need to understand the typical scenario here:
- High credit utilization suggests they're using a significant portion of their available credit
- Some high interest debt means they're likely paying substantial interest charges
- Credit score isn't "overly bad" - so they might be wondering about options like balance transfers or consolidation

The key considerations for attacking debt in this situation:

1. **Prioritization strategy**: With high interest debt, the mathematically optimal approach is usually to pay off the highest interest rate debt first (avalanche method). This minimizes total interest paid.

2. **The urgency factor**: High credit utilization combined with high interest rates creates a compounding problem - the debt grows while also hurting credit scores. This needs aggressive action.

3. **Balance transfer/consolidation question**: While the credit score might allow for some consolidation options, this can be a distraction from the core issue. If someone is in significant debt, getting more credit products often doesn't solve the underlying spending/income problem.

4. **Realistic expectations**: Paying off substantial debt requires lifestyle changes, potentially additional income, and sustained commitment. It's not a quick fix.

5. **Credit score improvement**: Interestingly, paying down debt aggressively will naturally improve credit utilization ratios, which is a major factor in credit scores. So focusing on debt payoff addresses both problems simultaneously.

The advice should emphasize:
- Treating all the debt with urgency
- Not getting distracted by consolidation schemes when aggressive payoff is the real solution
- Being realistic about the effort required
- Understanding that the credit score will improve as a byproduct of paying down debt
- The psychological/emotional component of debt payoff - commitment level matters

The person seems to be asking about whether to prioritize certain debts, whether consolidation is worth pursuing, and what timeline they're looking at. The answer should address all these concerns while keeping them focused on the fundamental task: paying off the debt as aggressively as possible.
</think>

ground_truth: Your bank has discretion to honor checks after 6 months, so you should talk to your bank about their specific policy. In general, banks won't accept "large" stale checks. The meaning of "large" varies -- $25,000 in NYC, as little as $2k in other places. Banks that service high-volume check issuers (like rebate companies) reject checks at 180 days. For business purposes, I think some banks will create accounts for specific mailings or other purposes as well. (i.e. 2011 refund account) The accounts close after a year.

The user is asking about adding a 180 day expiration to checks. This seems to be a question about check validity periods and banking policies.

Let me think through what I know about check expiration:

1. In the United States, the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) states that banks are not obligated to honor checks that are more than 6 months old (which is approximately 180 days). However, this doesn't mean banks are prohibited from honoring them - they have discretion.

2. The 6-month/180-day rule is a guideline, not an absolute requirement. Banks can choose to honor older checks if they want to.

3. Different banks have different policies regarding stale-dated checks. Some may be more strict, especially for larger amounts.

4. For businesses that issue many checks (like rebate companies), there are often specific arrangements with banks to reject checks after a certain period.

5. The threshold for what constitutes a "large" check that would be scrutinized more carefully varies by location and institution.

So the person asking about "adding a 180 day expiration to checks" is likely either:
- A business owner wanting to ensure their checks expire after 180 days
- Someone curious about check expiration policies
- Someone looking to implement a policy for their business

The response should address:
- That banks have discretion on honoring checks after 6 months
- The variability in policies between banks
- The concept of "large" checks being treated differently
- Special arrangements that businesses can make with banks
- The option of creating specific accounts for certain purposes that close after a set period

This covers the practical aspects of implementing or understanding a 180-day check expiration policy.

The question is about implementing a 180-day expiration policy for checks. Let me think through the relevant banking practices and policies.

First, I should consider the standard rules around check validity. Under the Uniform Commercial Code, banks generally aren't obligated to honor checks older than 6 months (roughly 180 days), but they have discretion - they're not prohibited from doing so either.

The key insight here is that this isn't a one-size-fits-all situation. Different banks handle stale-dated checks differently based on their own policies. The amount of the check matters significantly - banks tend to be more cautious with larger amounts. What counts as "large" varies geographically and by institution - major financial centers like NYC might set thresholds around $25,000, while smaller markets might flag checks as low as $2,000.

For businesses that issue high volumes of checks - like rebate companies that send out thousands of small checks - banks often have specific arrangements to automatically reject checks at the 180-day mark. This makes sense operationally since many rebate checks go uncashed.

There's also the option of purpose-specific accounts. Some banks will set up dedicated accounts for specific check-issuing purposes (like annual refunds) that automatically close after a defined period, effectively enforcing the expiration.

The practical advice here is that anyone wanting to implement a 180-day check expiration should consult directly with their bank about available options and specific policies, since there's significant variation in how different institutions handle this.

ground_truth: The car deal makes money 3 ways. If you pay in one lump payment. If the payment is greater than what they paid for the car, plus their expenses, they make a profit. They loan you the money. You make payments over months or years, if the total amount you pay is greater than what they paid for the car, plus their expenses, plus their finance expenses they make money. Of course the money takes years to come in, or they sell your loan to another business to get the money faster but in a smaller amount. You trade in a car and they sell it at a profit. Of course that new transaction could be a lump sum or a loan on the used car... They or course make money if you bring the car back for maintenance, or you buy lots of expensive dealer options. Some dealers wave two deals in front of you: get a 0% interest loan. These tend to be shorter 12 months vs 36,48,60 or even 72 months. The shorter length makes it harder for many to afford. If you can't swing the 12 large payments they offer you at x% loan for y years that keeps the payments in your budget. pay cash and get a rebate. If you take the rebate you can't get the 0% loan. If you take the 0% loan you can't get the rebate. The price you negotiate minus the rebate is enough to make a profit. The key is not letting them know which offer you are interested in. Don't even mention a trade in until the price of the new car has been finalized. Otherwise they will adjust the price, rebate, interest rate, length of loan,  and trade-in value to maximize their profit. The suggestion of running the numbers through a spreadsheet is a good one. If you get a loan for 2% from your bank/credit union for 3 years and the rebate from the dealer, it will cost less in total than the 0% loan from the dealer. The key is to get the loan approved by the bank/credit union before meeting with the dealer. The money from the bank looks like cash to the dealer.

generation.raw_text:
<think>
The user is asking about scams related to 0% financing vs rebate offers for cars. Let me think through this carefully.

First, I need to understand the context. Car dealerships often offer two mutually exclusive deals:
1. 0% financing (no interest on the loan)
2. A cash rebate (discount off the purchase price)

The question asks about "scams" - but I think this is really asking about how dealers structure these deals to maximize their profit, and how consumers can be misled or disadvantaged.

Let me think through the mechanics:

1. How dealers make money on car sales:
- The difference between what they paid for the car and what you pay
- Financing charges if they provide the loan
- Trade-in profit (buying your old car low, selling it higher)
- Service, maintenance, add-ons

2. The 0% vs rebate "scam" or manipulation:
- 0% financing sounds great - no interest! But these loans are typically shorter term (12-24 months vs 48-72 months), meaning higher monthly payments
- If you can't afford the higher payments, they push you toward a longer loan with interest
- The rebate is often substantial, but you can't combine it with 0% financing
- The dealer structures these so either option is profitable for them

3. The manipulation tactics:
- If you reveal which option you prefer early, they can adjust other variables (price, trade-in value) to maximize profit
- They might inflate the car price knowing you'll take the rebate
- They might offer less on your trade-in if you're getting 0% financing
- The 0% financing is subsidized by the manufacturer, but the dealer still makes money on the sale price

4. Consumer protection strategies:
- Don't reveal your financing preference until the price is negotiated
- Don't mention trade-in until the new car price is set
- Get pre-approved for a loan from a bank/credit union
- Run the numbers: sometimes a bank loan at 2-3% plus the rebate costs less total than 0% financing
- The bank loan looks like "cash" to the dealer

So the response should explain how these deals work, how dealers can manipulate them, and how consumers can protect themselves by understanding the math and negotiating strategically.
</think>

Of course that new transaction could be a lump sum or a loan on the used car... They or course make money if you bring the car back for maintenance, or you buy lots of expensive dealer options. Some dealers wave two deals in front of you: get a 0% interest loan. These tend to be shorter 12 months vs 36,48,60 or even 72 months. The shorter length makes it harder for many to afford. If you can't swing the 12 large payments they offer you at x% loan for y years that keeps the payments in your budget. pay cash and get a rebate. If you take the rebate you can't get the 0% loan. If you take the 0% loan you can't get the rebate. The price you negotiate minus the rebate is enough to make a profit. The key is not letting them know which offer you are interested in. Don't even mention a trade in until the price of the new car has been finalized. Otherwise they will adjust the price, rebate, interest rate, length of loan,  and trade-in value to maximize their profit. The suggestion of running the numbers through a spreadsheet is a good one. If you get a loan for 2% from your bank/credit union for 3 years and the rebate from the dealer, it will cost less in total than the 0% loan from the dealer. The key is to get the loan approved by the bank/credit union before meeting with the dealer. The money from the bank looks like cash to the dealer.

ground_truth: Consider the futures market. Traders buy and sell gold futures, but very few contracts, relatively speaking, result in delivery. The contracts are sold, and "Open interest" dwindles to near zero most months as the final date approaches. The seller buys back his short position, the buyer sells off his longs.  When I own a call, and am 'winning,' say the option that cost me $1 is now worth $2, I'd rather sell that option for even $1.95 than to buy 100 shares of a $148 stock. The punchline is that very few option buyers actually hope to own the stock in the end. Just like the futures, open interest falls as expiration approaches.

generation.raw_text:
<think>
The user is asking about a seemingly paradoxical statistic: if only ~10% of options expire worthless and only ~10% are exercised, what happens to the other ~80%?

Let me think through this logically.

Options have three possible outcomes at expiration:
1. Expire worthless (out of the money)
2. Get exercised (in the money and holder chooses to exercise)
3. ???

The key insight here is that options don't have to be held until expiration. They can be closed out before expiration by selling the option (if you're long) or buying it back (if you're short).

So the breakdown would be:
- ~10% expire worthless
- ~10% are exercised
- ~80% are closed out before expiration (sold/bought back)

This makes sense when you think about why people trade options:
1. Most option traders are speculating on price movement, not trying to actually acquire or sell the underlying stock
2. If you bought a call option and it's profitable, you can simply sell the option to capture the profit rather than exercising it
3. Exercising requires more capital (buying 100 shares at the strike price) and you lose any remaining time value
4. It's more capital efficient to just sell the option

This is analogous to futures markets where most contracts are closed out before delivery rather than resulting in actual physical delivery of the commodity. A gold futures trader doesn't actually want to take delivery of gold bars - they want to profit from price movements.

The "open interest" concept is key here - open interest represents the total number of outstanding contracts. As expiration approaches, open interest typically decreases because traders are closing their positions rather than holding to expiration.

So the answer to the apparent paradox is that the vast majority of options (~80%) are simply closed out before expiration through offsetting trades, leaving only a small percentage to either expire worthless or be exercised.

The question presents what seems like a mathematical impossibility: if only ~10% expire worthless and only ~10% are exercised, what happens to the remaining ~80%?

The key insight is that options don't have to be held until expiration. The missing ~80% represents options that are **closed out before expiration** through offsetting trades.

Here's the reasoning:

When someone owns a profitable call option, they have two choices:
1. Exercise it - which requires significant capital to buy 100 shares at the strike price
2. Sell the option itself - which captures the profit with far less capital

Most traders choose option 2. If I bought a call for $1 and it's now worth $2, I'd rather sell it for $1.95 than tie up capital buying 100 shares of stock. I capture nearly all the profit without the capital requirement.

This parallels the futures market perfectly. Gold futures traders buy and sell contracts, but very few actually want physical delivery of gold. As expiration approaches, traders close their positions - sellers buy back their shorts, buyers sell their longs - and open interest drops dramatically.

The same dynamic applies to options:
- Most option traders are speculating on price movement, not seeking stock ownership
- Closing a position is more capital-efficient than exercising
- Open interest naturally decreases as expiration approaches

So the ~80% of options that neither expire worthless nor get exercised are simply closed out through offsetting trades before expiration day arrives.

When considering replacements for Microsoft Money, there are various approaches - from commercial software like Quicken to free and open-source options. For users who are comfortable with different paradigms, there are also command-line based double-entry accounting tools.

A related tool is ledger (available at the GitHub wiki), which follows a similar philosophy but is command-line only.

These tools represent a different approach compared to Microsoft Money's traditional GUI-based personal finance management. They're based on plain-text accounting principles and double-entry bookkeeping. While they have a learning curve and aren't for everyone, they offer advantages like data portability, transparency, and powerful querying capabilities that some users find valuable.

The response acknowledges that these alternatives are "different" from what users might expect coming from Microsoft Money, but suggests they're worth exploring for certain users who might appreciate their particular strengths.

ground_truth: The actual price is represented on charts and not the change in price as a percentage, because it is the actual price which is used in all other parts of analysis (both technical and fundamental), and it is the actual figure the security is bought and sold at. A change in price has to be relative to a previous price at a previous time, and we can easily work out the change in price over any given time period. I think what you are concerned about is how to compare a certain actual price change in low priced securities to the same actual price change in a higher priced securities.  For example: $1.00 rise in a $2.00 stock representing a 50% increase in price; $1.00 rise in a $10.00 stock representing a 10% increase in price. On a standard chart both of these look the same, as they both show a $1.00 increase in price. So what can we do to show the true representation of the percentage increase in price? It is actually quite simple. You view the chart using a log scale instead of a standard scale (most charting packages should have this option). What may look like a bubble on a standard scale chart, looks like a healthy uptrend on a log scale chart and represents a true picture of the percentage change in price. Example of Standard Price Scale VS LOG Price Scale on a Chart Standard Price Scale  On the standard scale the price seems to have very little movement from Mar09 to Jan12 and then the price seems to zoom up after Jan12 to Mar13. This is because a 4% increase (for example) of $0.50 is only $0.02, whilst a 4% increase of $7.00 $0.28, so the increases seem much bigger at the end of the chart. LOG Price Scale  On the LOG chart however, these price changes seem to be more evenly displayed no matter at what price level the price change has occurred at. This thus give a better representation of how fast or slow the price is rising or falling, or the size of the change in price.

generation.raw_text:
<think>
The user is asking why price graphs typically show actual prices rather than percent changes. Let me think through this question.

First, I need to consider why actual prices are used on charts:

1. **Practical trading reasons**: When you buy or sell a security, you're dealing with actual prices, not percentages. The actual price is what matters for order execution, calculating position sizes, and determining profit/loss in dollar terms.

2. **Technical analysis**: Many technical indicators and analysis methods (support/resistance levels, moving averages, trend lines, etc.) are based on actual price levels. These would be harder to interpret or calculate if charts showed percentage changes.

3. **Fundamental analysis**: Price-to-earnings ratios, book value comparisons, and other fundamental metrics relate to actual prices.

4. **Reference point problem**: A percentage change requires a reference point - change from what? Yesterday's close? Last week? Last year? The starting point would be arbitrary and different analysts might want different reference periods.

Now, the user might be concerned about comparing price movements across different securities or across time when prices are at different levels. For example:
- A $1 move in a $10 stock is 10%
- A $1 move in a $100 stock is only 1%

On a standard linear scale chart, both would appear as the same vertical distance, which doesn't accurately represent the relative significance of the move.

The solution to this problem is using a logarithmic (log) scale:
- On a log scale, equal vertical distances represent equal percentage changes
- This makes it easier to compare price movements at different price levels
- A stock that goes from $10 to $20 (100% gain) would show the same vertical distance as going from $100 to $200 (also 100% gain)

This is particularly useful for:
- Long-term charts where prices have changed significantly
- Comparing the rate of growth/decline at different price levels
- Avoiding the visual distortion where recent price movements look exaggerated compared to historical movements (when prices were lower)

So the answer involves explaining why actual prices are used (practical reasons) and then addressing the underlying concern about percentage representation by introducing the concept of logarithmic scale charts as the solution.
</think>

The actual price is represented on charts and not the change in price as a percentage, because it is the actual price which is used in all other parts of analysis (both technical and fundamental), and it is the actual figure the security is bought and sold at. A change in price has to be relative to a previous price at a previous time, and we can easily work out the change in price over any given time period.

I think what you are concerned about is how to compare a certain actual price change in low priced securities to the same actual price change in a higher priced securities. For example: $1.00 rise in a $2.00 stock representing a 50% increase in price; $1.00 rise in a $10.00 stock representing a 10% increase in price. On a standard chart both of these look the same, as they both show a $1.00 increase in price.

So what can we do to show the true representation of the percentage increase in price? It is actually quite simple. You view the chart using a log scale instead of a standard scale (most charting packages should have this option). What may look like a bubble on a standard scale chart, looks like a healthy uptrend on a log scale chart and represents a true picture of the percentage change in price.

On a standard scale, the price seems to have very little movement during periods when the stock was cheap, and then the price seems to zoom up when the stock is more expensive. This is because a 4% increase of $0.50 is only $0.02, whilst a 4% increase of $7.00 is $0.28, so the increases seem much bigger at higher price levels.

On a LOG chart however, these price changes seem to be more evenly displayed no matter at what price level the price change has occurred at. This gives a better representation of how fast or slow the price is rising or falling, or the relative size of the change in price.

ground_truth: The game is not zero sum. When a friend and I chop down a tree, and build a house from it, the house has value, far greater than the value of a standing tree. Our labor has turned into something of value.  In theory, a company starts from an idea, and offers either a good or service to create value. There are scams that make it seem like a Vegas casino. There are times a stock will trade for well above what it should. When I buy the S&P index at a fair price for 1000 (through an etf or fund) and years later it's 1400, the gain isn't out of someone else's pocket, else the amount of wealth in the world would be fixed and that's not the case. Over time, investors lag the market return for multiple reasons, trading costs, bad timing, etc. Statements such as "90% lose money" are hyperbole meant to separate you from your money. A self fulfilling prophesy.   The question of lagging the market is another story - I have no data to support my observation, but I'd imagine that well over 90% lag the broad market. A detailed explanation is too long for this forum, but simply put, there are trading costs. If I invest in an S&P ETF that costs .1% per year, I'll see a return of say 9.9% over decades if the market return is 10%. Over 40 years, this is 4364% compounded, vs the index 4526% compounded, a difference of less than 4% in final wealth. There are load funds that charge more than this just to buy in (5% anyone?).  Lagging by a small fraction is a far cry from 'losing money.'  There is an annual report by a company named Dalbar that tracks investor performance. For the 20 year period ending 12/31/10 the S&P returned 9.14% and Dalbar calculates the average investor had an average return of 3.83%. Pretty bad, but not zero. Since you don't cite a particular article or source, there may be more to the story. Day traders are likely to lose. As are a series of other types of traders in other markets, Forex for one.  While your question may be interesting, its premise of "many experts say...." without naming even one leaves room for doubt.  Note - I've updated the link for the 2015 report. And 4 years later, I see that when searching on that 90% statistic, the articles are about day traders. That actually makes sense to me.
